title: Parity-induced generalized Brillouin zone without non-Hermitian skin effect
abstract: Acute spectral sensitivity to boundary conditions and the formation of a generalized Brillouin zone associated with complex quasimomenta are features frequently attributed to systems with non-trivial non-Hermitian topology, showcasing the non-Hermitian skin effect. We show that, away from the thermodynamic limit, these features themselves are not uniquely tied to this phenomenon; they can similarly arise as parity-induced even-odd effects in non-Hermitian systems without skin effect. Despite an underlying generalized Brillouin zone description, wavefunctions remain delocalized. In addition, the effect can arise in skin-effect models as entirely separate distinguishable feature
summary: This work investigates spectral features in non-Hermitian quantum systems, specifically challenging the notion that complex quasimomenta and generalized Brillouin zones always imply the non-Hermitian skin effect. By analyzing the SSH* model, the authors prove that these complex features can instead originate from parity-induced even-odd effects inherent to finite chain lengths, even when the system remains fundamentally delocalized.
main_problem: To demonstrate that spectral sensitivity and the formation of a generalized Brillouin zone (GBZ) can occur in non-Hermitian systems due to parity-induced even-odd effects, even when the conventional non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) is absent.
main_result: The study shows that the complex quasimomentum structure and GBZ deviation can arise purely from finite-size parity effects in the SSH* model, independent of the NHSE, while wavefunctions remain delocalized.
method: The analysis employs the generalized Brillouin zone formalism, analyzing the Hamiltonian's spectrum for finite chains of odd and even lengths using symmetry classification (Altland-Zirnbauer classes).
model_or_system: The primary model is the SSH* tight-binding Hamiltonian, which is a non-Hermitian generalization of the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model, studied on finite chains of length N.
key_observables: Eigenvalue spectra, generalized Brillouin zone structure, Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and the comparison between open vs. periodic boundary conditions.
important_parameters: Chain length N (odd vs. even), the coupling strengths t and delta, and the thermodynamic limit (N -> infinity).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on constructing specific compound states to satisfy eigenvalue relations and distinguishing between parity-induced effects and the true NHSE.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate eigenvalue spectra comparing periodic and open boundary conditions for both the Hatano-Nelson model (showing NHSE) and the SSH* model (showing parity-induced GBZ deviations).
paper_structure: The paper systematically contrasts systems exhibiting NHSE with the SSH* model, deriving the GBZ for finite chains, and finally separating the observed spectral sensitivity into contributions from parity effects versus the NHSE.
why_interesting: It provides a crucial theoretical distinction between two superficially similar phenomena—spectral sensitivity due to finite-size parity effects versus the true non-Hermitian skin effect—which is vital for interpreting experimental measurements in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Features of HHN. Top: Eigenvalue spectra under periodic (blue) and open (red) boundary conditions in the complex energy plane. Bottom: Macroscopic localization of bulk states under open boundaries, displaying the non-Hermitian skin effect.
Figure 2: Schematic illustration of the TRS† and TRS symmetries in finite-chain realizations of HSSH∗at odd (top) and even (bottom) chain length.

Figure 3: Spectrum of HSSH∗with t = δ in the complex energy plane. Shown is the Bloch spectrum (blue), an illustrative example of the eigenvalues obtained for odd chain lengths (black), and eigenvalues found for various even chain lengths (red) displaying an acute sensitivity to the boundary conditions. Red lines indicate the non-Bloch bands along the reconstructed generalized Brillouin zone found at even chain lengths.

Figure 4: Complex quasimomenta of the generalized Brillouin zone description for the HSSH∗model at even chain lengths and t = δ. The behavior of Im k is amplitude suppressed with increasing chain lengths, but remains structurally unchanged. The reconstructed shape of the generalized Brillouin zone is indicated as red lines. The conventional Brillouin zone is indicated in blue for reference.

Fig. 3. Clearly, this behavior is no longer captured by the conventional band spectrum (10) found under peri- odic boundaries. It marks an acute parity-induced sen- sitivity to the boundary conditions, reminiscent of the non-Hermitian skin effect. Moreover, and curiously in light of the absence of a closed-loop spectral structure under periodic boundaries, the analysis within the generalized Brillouin zone formal- ism identifies a consistent standing-wave construction for the description of bulk states at kj ∈C with Rekj ∈(0,π) satisfying
